sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Christopher Watson, an 86-year-old male, returns in follow-up for adult hydrocephalus.
He presents with a history of progressive gait impairment, cognitive impairment, and decreased bladder control for 8-10 years.
He underwent shunt surgery on August 1st, 2020, with a Medtronic Strata programmable shunt in the ventricular peritoneal configuration set at a level 2.0.
Since surgery, he has experienced some improvements in gait and bladder control, but still requires assistance with walking and has urinary incontinence.
He denies headaches, fever, or unintentional weight loss.
Past medical history: Not mentioned.
Review of systems: Gait impairment, cognitive impairment, urinary incontinence, and wearing a diaper.
No fever, unintentional weight loss, genital sores, decreased or painful urination, headaches, recent falls, or hallucinations.
Current medications: Not mentioned.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 124/80, heart rate 64, respirations 18, weight 174 pounds, and pain 0 out of 10.
Physical exam: The shunt site was clean, dry, and intact with a confirmed setting of 2.0.
Gait testing using the Tinetti assessment tool showed a score of 6-8 out of 12 and a balance score of 12 out of 16 for a total score of 18-20 out of 28.
Cranial nerves, pupils, and eoms were intact.
Face was symmetric.
Motor was normal for bulk and strength, but coordination was slow from finger to nose.
CT scan from 10/15/2020 showed a frontal horn span at the level of foramen of Monro of 4.6 centimeters with a third ventricular contour that is flat with a span of 10 millimeters.
There was a tiny amount of blood in the right front region with a tiny subdermal collection, and substantial small vessel ischemic change.
Test Results: CT scan from 10/15/2020 shows a frontal horn span at the level of foramen of Monro of 4.6 centimeters with a third ventricular contour that is flat with a span of 10 millimeters.
There is a tiny amount of blood in the right front region with a tiny subdermal collection, and substantial small vessel ischemic change.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient is a pleasant and cooperative 86-year-old male with adult hydrocephalus, gait impairment, urinary incontinence and urgency, and cognitive impairment.
Problem list: Adult hydrocephalus; Gait impairment; Urinary incontinence and urgency; Cognitive impairment; Status: confirmed.
Plan:
I will leave the shunt set for another 2-3 months to monitor for any changes.
The patient will return in 3 months for follow-up.
I will also discuss the possibility of driver's safety program with the patient, and he can enroll in it without my recommendation.
Follow-up: The patient will return in 3 months for follow-up.
